questions.question: X-rays and gamma rays are called 'indirectly ionizing' because:
options: They ionize only after decaying into charged particles
Most of the ionization is produced by the fast electrons their interactions liberate, not by the photons themselves
They are unable to ionize matter at all
They ionize only in air, never in metal
questions.explanation: A photon interacts in a single event and hands its energy to an electron; that fast electron then creates nearly all the ion pairs along its track.

questions.question: Immediately after a photoelectric interaction, the absorbing atom typically emits:
options: A characteristic X-ray as the inner-shell vacancy is filled
questions.explanation: The ejected photoelectron leaves an inner-shell vacancy; an outer electron drops in to fill it and the energy difference is emitted as a characteristic X-ray.

questions.question: In Compton scattering, the energy of the scattered photon:
options: Decreases as the scattering angle increases — a photon scattered straight back has lost the most energy it can
Is always equal to the incident photon's energy
questions.explanation: The photon shares energy with the recoil electron, and the share given up grows with scattering angle, so large-angle and backscattered photons emerge with the lowest energies.

questions.question: The two photons produced when a positron annihilates with an electron carry:
options: 0.511 MeV each, emitted in nearly opposite directions
Whatever energy the original photon carried
questions.explanation: Annihilation converts the two rest masses back into energy: two photons of 0.511 MeV each, traveling nearly back-to-back.

questions.question: Exposure, whether stated in C/kg or roentgens, is a defined quantity only for:
options: All ionizing radiation in any material
X- and gamma radiation, in air
questions.explanation: Exposure quantifies ionization charge liberated in dry air by photons only; absorbed dose and dose equivalent are the quantities that extend to other radiations and materials.

questions.question: What is the approximate unshielded exposure rate at 1 m from a 120 Ci Ir-192 source?
questions.explanation: Ir-192 emits about 0.5 R/h per Ci at 1 m, so 0.5 × 120 = 60 R/h. (156 R/h would be a 120 Ci Co-60 source at ≈ 1.3 R/h per Ci.)

questions.question: Why does each X-ray machine need its own emission chart, while a gamma source needs only a decay chart?
options: X-ray output is perfectly predictable from physics alone
Gamma source output fluctuates day to day
Emission charts are only a regulatory formality
X-ray output depends on kV, mA, filtration, target, and waveform — machine-specific factors — while a given isotope's per-curie emissivity is fixed by its decay scheme
questions.explanation: A radionuclide's photon output per curie is set by nature, so only activity (decay) must be tracked; an X-ray tube's output depends on its controls and construction, so it is documented per machine.

questions.question: A source produces 16 R/h at 1 m. At what distance does the unshielded rate fall to 1 R/h?
questions.explanation: I₁d₁² = I₂d₂² gives d₂ = √(16 × 1² ÷ 1) = 4 m — a factor of 16 in intensity needs a factor of 4 in distance.

questions.question: A Co-60 beam (HVL ≈ 12.5 mm in lead) reads 60 R/h. Behind 25 mm of lead, the narrow-beam estimate is:
questions.explanation: 25 mm is two half-value layers, a reduction factor of 2² = 4: 60 ÷ 4 = 15 R/h.

questions.question: A radiographer moves from 4 m to 2 m from an exposed source. The dose rate:
questions.explanation: Halving the distance multiplies the rate by four — the inverse square law working against you, which is why the last steps of an approach are surveyed continuously.
